title: “Worthless Idiot, Donkey Head”: Parodies of Pedantry on the Renaissance Stage
content: Pompous know-it-alls were once a mainstay of mockery on the Italian stage. Arnoud Visser investigates this stock character of the pedant and his association with individual superiority, social distinction, and sexual transgression, finding a form of satire that took aim at the Renaissance humanist’s erudition and lofty ideals.
snippet: Pompous know-it-alls were once a mainstay of mockery on the Italian stage. Arnoud Visser investigates this stock character of the pedant and his association with individual superiority, social

title: Peruvianizing Peru: Covers from Amauta Magazine (1926–30)
content: Cover designs for José Carlos Mariátegui’s radical Lima-based magazine.
snippet: Cover designs for José Carlos Mariátegui’s radical Lima-based magazine.

title: Choice Notes on History from Notes and Queries (1858)
content: Selections from the long-running British journal on historical peculiarities, from the whereabouts of Mary Queen of Scot’s severed head to the identity of Charles I's hooded executioner.
snippet: Selections from the long-running British journal on historical peculiarities, from the whereabouts of Mary Queen of Scot’s severed head to the identity of Charles I's hooded executioner.

snippet: Diagrams from a now-lost Urdu manuscript illustrating the propitious days and directions for travel.

title: Louis Pope Gratacap, A Curator in Lost Worlds
content: Arguably the first work of fiction to feature a Tyrannosaurus rex, Louis Pope Gratacap’s The New Northland (1915) is at once kaleidoscopic, mischievous, fascinating — and exhausting. Richard Fallon explores this “lost world” novel, finding a work as interested in cutting-edge science as it was in paying dues to its generic precursors.
snippet: Arguably the first work of fiction to feature a Tyrannosaurus rex, Louis Pope Gratacap’s The New Northland (1915) is at once kaleidoscopic, mischievous, fascinating — and exhausting. Richard Fallon

title: The Art of Kite Flying (1430–1929)
content: 69 images of kite flying from across 5 centuries and some dozen countries.
snippet: 69 images of kite flying from across 5 centuries and some dozen countries.

title: Venetian Bridge Brawls in 17th and 18th Century Art
content: Artworks from an era when Venetians brawled on bridges.

title: Animal, Vegetable, Lamb: The Zoophyte from Tartary
content: In the Middle Ages, rumours spread across Europe of a strange hybrid creature, half-animal, half-plant, known as the Vegetable Lamb of Tartary. A hoax based on fern rhizomes? A euphemism for culled fetal lambs? Or perhaps a medieval misapprehension of cotton? Thom Sliwowski traces the roots of this fabled zoophyte.
snippet: In the Middle Ages, rumours spread across Europe of a strange hybrid creature, half-animal, half-plant, known as the Vegetable Lamb of Tartary. A hoax based on fern rhizomes? A euphemism for culled

title: Twilight of the Velocipede: Typesetting Races before the Age of Linotype
content: Before Linotype revolutionised typesetting in the 1880s, compositors set texts by hand — and they set them fast. Alex Wright rediscovers the thrilling world of typesetting races, which drew crowds in the thousands, offered huge cash prizes, and helped women "Swifts" fight for workplace equity.
snippet: Before Linotype revolutionised typesetting in the 1880s, compositors set texts by hand — and they set them fast. Alex Wright rediscovers the thrilling world of typesetting races, which drew crowds in

title: Magic by Return of Post: How Mail Order Delivered the Occult
content: What allowed occultism to blossom in the United States at the turn of the 20th century? Linotype machines, cheap pulp paper, and newly improved postal networks. Allan Johnson investigates the forgotten history and (still living) world of mail-order magic.

title: The Great Majority: Body Snatching and Burial Reform in 19th-Century Britain
content: As populations flocked to city centres in the 19th century, church cemeteries began to overflow with the dead. Roger Luckhurst exhumes the history of this period, when anatomists fuelled a body-snatching trade led by “resurrection men” and reformers sought alternatives to the toxic urban graveyards and their pestilent fumes.
snippet: As populations flocked to city centres in the 19th century, church cemeteries began to overflow with the dead. Roger Luckhurst exhumes the history of this period, when anatomists fuelled a

content: Marvels, wonders, and monstrosities: something between a medieval bestiary and a scientific treatise on birth defects.
snippet: Marvels, wonders, and monstrosities: something between a medieval bestiary and a scientific treatise on birth defects.

snippet: Moneymaking caricatures by a teenage Monet, before he turned to Impressionism.

title: Fear and Fragility: The Glass Delusion and Its History
content: In early modern Europe, around the time when lenses began to bring the world (and heavens) into newfound focus, patients started appearing in medical records with a particular ailment: a firm belief that they were made of glass. Tamara Sanderson investigates the source and manifestation of this delusion, and finds a psychological idiom that once carried the weight of what could otherwise not be said.
snippet: In early modern Europe, around the time when lenses began to bring the world (and heavens) into newfound focus, patients started appearing in medical records with a particular ailment: a firm belief

content: A utopian novel where men are no longer necessary.

content: Illustrations of Javanese wayang kulit puppets with vivid colour and gilding.
snippet: Illustrations of Javanese wayang kulit puppets with vivid colour and gilding.

title: The Blinkered Flâneur: Walking with Franz Hessel in 1920s Berlin
content: Does the flâneur, that curiously modern figure who wanders metropolitan streets, have a political consciousness? For Franz Hessel — author of Spazieren in Berlin, “a memorization while strolling” that Walter Benjamin called “thoroughly epic” — the answer seemed to be no. Paul Sullivan explores Hessel’s perambulations through Berlin and the achievements and limitations of his vision.
snippet: Does the flâneur, that curiously modern figure who wanders metropolitan streets, have a political consciousness? For Franz Hessel — author of Spazieren in Berlin, “a memorization while strolling” that

content: Unruly steeds from King Mu’s mythology, cavorting across a silk scroll.
snippet: Unruly steeds from King Mu’s mythology, cavorting across a silk scroll.

title: Pigeons Transmogrified: Hannah Glasse’s Art of Cookery (1751 edition)

title: Snail Homes, Bog Bodies, and Mechanical Flies: Robert Testard’s Illustrations for Les secretz de l’histoire naturelle (ca. 1485)

title: Typing for Love or Money: The Hidden Women’s Labor behind Modern Literary Masterpieces
content: Taking dictation, revising manuscripts, typing copies, literary amanuenses often labour for little compensation and even less recognition. Christine Jacobson explores the neglected efforts of women like Theodora Bosanquet, Véra Nabokov, and Valerie Eliot, who — through their work as typists, editors, and champions — had a profound impact on modern literature.
snippet: Taking dictation, revising manuscripts, typing copies, literary amanuenses often labour for little compensation and even less recognition. Christine Jacobson explores the neglected efforts of women

title: Cybernetic Attention: All Watched over by Machines We Learned to Watch
content: Before the attention economy consumed our lives, “pursuit tests” devised by the US military coupled man to machine with the aim of assessing focus under pressure. D. Graham Burnett explores these devices for evaluating aviators, finding a pre-history of the laboratory research that has relentlessly worked to slice and dice the attentional powers of human beings.
snippet: Before the attention economy consumed our lives, “pursuit tests” devised by the US military coupled man to machine with the aim of assessing focus under pressure. D. Graham Burnett explores these

title: Happy Public Domain Day 2026!
content: Each January 1st is Public Domain Day, when a new crop of works have their copyrights expire and become free to share and reuse for any purpose. Here's our highlights for 2026.

content: From sublime spheres to hungry cats, a rundown of the ten most read of what we published this year.
snippet: From sublime spheres to hungry cats, a rundown of the ten most read of what we published this year.

title: A Thousand and One Nights in Italy: The Moorish Fantasias of Cesare Mattei and Ferdinando Panciatichi
content: In mid-19th century Italy, two eccentric aristocrats set forth on parallel projects: constructing ostentatious castles in a Moorish Revival style. Iván Moure Pazos tours the psychedelic chambers of Rochetta Mattei, optimised for electrohomeopathic healing, and Castello di Sammezzano, an immersive, orientalist fever dream.
snippet: In mid-19th century Italy, two eccentric aristocrats set forth on parallel projects: constructing ostentatious castles in a Moorish Revival style. Iván Moure Pazos tours the psychedelic chambers of

content: French lithographs of the Eiffel Tower and its environs, in the style of Japanese woodblock prints.
snippet: French lithographs of the Eiffel Tower and its environs, in the style of Japanese woodblock prints.

title: Art in Art: Cabinets of Curiosity and the Rise of the Gallery Painting
content: In the 17th century, emanating from Antwerp, a new genre of artwork came on the scene: paintings of paintings, works populated by a lush array of meta-images. From its origins in picturing private curiosity cabinets to its later use in documenting increasingly public collections, Thea Applebaum Licht charts the course of this alluring aesthetic tradition.
snippet: In the 17th century, emanating from Antwerp, a new genre of artwork came on the scene: paintings of paintings, works populated by a lush array of meta-images. From its origins in picturing private

content: A past vision of the future. Domestic utopia? Or sanitised hell?
snippet: A past vision of the future. Domestic utopia? Or sanitised hell?

snippet: Adorn your body and coffee in PDR goodness! We’ve just added 8 new T-shirts and 13 new mugs to our online shop.

title: Roma Lister, Aradia, and the Speculative Origins of a Witchcraft Revival
content: In 1899, Charles Godfrey Leland published Aradia, “the gospel of the witches”, containing a goddess-orientated creation and saviour narrative, purported to descend from an ancient, hermetic tradition of witchcraft in Italy. A. D. Manns explores this text via an enchanting conjecture: that the writer, medium, and witch Roma Lister played a pivotal role in the formation of both Aradia and, therefore, a new form of paganism called Wicca.
snippet: In 1899, Charles Godfrey Leland published Aradia, “the gospel of the witches”, containing a goddess-orientated creation and saviour narrative, purported to descend from an ancient, hermetic tradition

title: “That’s Why We Become Witches”: Sylvia Townsend Warner’s Lolly Willowes (1926)
content: A novel about a woman who throws off the yoke of patriarchy to become a witch.
snippet: A novel about a woman who throws off the yoke of patriarchy to become a witch.

title: Hooked on Sonics: Experimenting with Sound in 19th-Century Popular Science
content: Of all the senses cultivated throughout the 19th century, it was the sense of hearing that experienced the most dramatic transformation, as the science of sound underwent rapid advancement. Lucas Thompson delves into a particular genre of popular acoustics primers aimed at children and amateurs alike, which reveal the pedagogical, ludic, and transcendental strivings of Victorian society.
snippet: Of all the senses cultivated throughout the 19th century, it was the sense of hearing that experienced the most dramatic transformation, as the science of sound underwent rapid advancement. Lucas

content: A medical tract on the health effects of burying oneself alive in mud.
snippet: A medical tract on the health effects of burying oneself alive in mud.

title: Paramaribo and the Plantation: Gerrit Schouten’s Dioramas of Suriname (1810–30)
content: Dioramas avant la lettre that depict local life in Suriname.
snippet: Dioramas avant la lettre that depict local life in Suriname.

content: Illustrations of supposed physiognomic affinities between humans and animals.
snippet: Illustrations of supposed physiognomic affinities between humans and animals.

title: The Adventures and Experiences of the First Slovak Novel
content: Partially banned upon publication and translated into English for the first time this year, René, or: A Young Man’s Adventures and Experiences (1783–85) found new readers in the communist era thanks to its critiques of feudalism, capitalism, and the Catholic Church. Dobrota Pucherová introduces us to this hybrid work, which mixes the bildungsroman with the philosophical novel, the romance, the adventure story, the travelogue, the history book, and the orientalist fantasy.
snippet: Partially banned upon publication and translated into English for the first time this year, René, or: A Young Man’s Adventures and Experiences (1783–85) found new readers in the communist era thanks

content: Expedition accounts of aeronauts bravely venturing into the heavens on hot-air balloons.
snippet: Expedition accounts of aeronauts bravely venturing into the heavens on hot-air balloons.

content: A "living picture" film staging Botticelli’s Birth of Venus with a twist.

content: A Rapture that wasn’t, in 2025 and 1844.

content: Paintings illustrating Pliny the Elder’s account of the origins of art.

title: “You Think Me a Bold Cheat”: Mary Carleton, Counterfeit Princess
content: Accused of posing as foreign royalty to lure her young suitor into a bigamous marriage, Mary Carleton was the subject of dozens of pamphlets and broadsides published in the mid-17th century, including by Carleton herself. Investigating the fraudster’s life, Laura Kolb finds a self-fashioning figure who both influenced the emergence of the English novel and serves as a strange precursor to our modern-day fascination with conwomen and counterfeits, like the heiress manqué Anna Delvey.
snippet: Accused of posing as foreign royalty to lure her young suitor into a bigamous marriage, Mary Carleton was the subject of dozens of pamphlets and broadsides published in the mid-17th century, including

title: A Collection of Old English Customs, and Curious Bequests and Charities (1842)
content: A compilation of historical gifting traditions in England, with a focus on the peculiar.
snippet: A compilation of historical gifting traditions in England, with a focus on the peculiar.

title: Ivan Aivazovsky’s Miniature Seascapes (ca. 1887)
content: Identical photographs of the artist, each with a unique miniature painting at the centre.

content: A manuscript that pairs illustrations of cats with poetic descriptions and notes on what mystical benefits their owners might hope to accrue.

title: Watching the World in a Dark Room: The Early Modern Camera Obscura
content: Centuries before photography froze the world into neat frames, scientists, poets, and artists streamed transient images into dark interior spaces with the help of a camera obscura. Julie Park explores the early modern fascination with this quasi-spiritual technology and the magic, melancholy, and dream-like experiences it produced.
snippet: Centuries before photography froze the world into neat frames, scientists, poets, and artists streamed transient images into dark interior spaces with the help of a camera obscura. Julie Park explores

snippet: Commemorative manuscript featuring illustrations of pageants, costumes, and fireworks, later further illustrated by a separate artist, with floral motifs.

content: Female astronomical first, in 2025 and 1787.
snippet: Female astronomical first, in 2025 and 1787.

title: Splitting Hairs: Chinese Immigrants, the Queue, and the Boundaries of Political Citizenship
content: As Chinese immigration to California accelerated across the 19th century, the hairstyle known as the queue — a long, braided pony tail — became the subject of white Americans’ fascination, disgust, and legal regulation. Sarah Gold McBride explores why hair served as an index of political subjecthood, and how the queue exposed cracks in American norms regarding gender, economy, and citizenship.
snippet: As Chinese immigration to California accelerated across the 19th century, the hairstyle known as the queue — a long, braided pony tail — became the subject of white Americans’ fascination, disgust,

content: A crop circle sighting tinged with Christian morality.
snippet: A crop circle sighting tinged with Christian morality.

title: Perspiration, Bilocation, and Plagiarisation: “The Heat Wave” (1929)
content: Time travel and murder during a New York heat wave.
snippet: Time travel and murder during a New York heat wave.

content: Excerpts from a dazzling image collection, discovered in a Norwegian barn in the 1980s, that experiments with the presentation of gender.
snippet: Excerpts from a dazzling image collection, discovered in a Norwegian barn in the 1980s, that experiments with the presentation of gender.

title: Dancing Naked on the Head of a Pin: The Early History of Microphotography
content: In 1853, John Benjamin Dancer achieved a feat of seemingly impossible scale: he shrunk an image to the size of a sharpened pencil tip. Anika Burgess explores the invention of microphotography and its influence on erotic paraphernalia and military communications.
snippet: In 1853, John Benjamin Dancer achieved a feat of seemingly impossible scale: he shrunk an image to the size of a sharpened pencil tip. Anika Burgess explores the invention of microphotography and its

content: Depictions of the mythical creatures known as Blemmyes: humanoids whose eyes, nose, and mouth are embedded in their breast.
snippet: Depictions of the mythical creatures known as Blemmyes: humanoids whose eyes, nose, and mouth are embedded in their breast.

title: Imagining an Idle Countess: George Wightwick’s The Palace of Architecture
content: In 1840, British architect George Wightwick published a world history of architecture in the Romantic mode, inviting readers to enter a vast garden where Buddhist iconography rubs shoulders with Greek temples and Egyptian pyramids gaze upon Gothic cathedrals. His intended audience? Idle women. Matthew Mullane revisits this visionary but ultimately unpopular text, revealing the legacy of attempts to gatekeep the realms of imagination and fantasy pertaining to the built environment.
snippet: In 1840, British architect George Wightwick published a world history of architecture in the Romantic mode, inviting readers to enter a vast garden where Buddhist iconography rubs shoulders with Greek

content: A set of drawing workbooks embroiled in debates about the Indigenist aesthetic movement.

content: A series of conjectures about the primal scene of writing.

content: A stunning six-foot-long map that joins the worlds of various myths and stories for the childhood adventurer.

title: The Old, Old, Very Old Man: Thomas Parr and the Longevity Trade
content: As the story goes, Old Tom Parr was relatively healthy for being 152 until a visit to noxious, polluted London in 1635 cut his long life short. Katherine Harvey investigates the early modern claims surrounding this supercentarian and the fraudulent longevity business that became his namesake in the 19th century.
snippet: As the story goes, Old Tom Parr was relatively healthy for being 152 until a visit to noxious, polluted London in 1635 cut his long life short. Katherine Harvey investigates the early modern claims

title: Old Tricks: Tiny Cryptic #9
content: Ninth instalment in our series of extremely small and free-form cryptic crossword puzzles, themed on our latest essay.
snippet: Ninth instalment in our series of extremely small and free-form cryptic crossword puzzles, themed on our latest essay.

title: “The Great Enigma of Our Times”: Henry George’s Poverty and Progress (1881 edition)
content: A landmark work of social reform — proposing a land value tax — that helped usher in the Progressive Era.
snippet: A landmark work of social reform — proposing a land value tax — that helped usher in the Progressive Era.

title: In the Penal Colony: Now and Then #3
content: French overseas imprisonment, in 2025 and 1852.
snippet: French overseas imprisonment, in 2025 and 1852.

title: Gilded Fish: Illustrations from Histoire naturelle des dorades de la Chine (1780)
content: Stunning illustrations from the first monograph on goldfish published in Europe.
snippet: Stunning illustrations from the first monograph on goldfish published in Europe.

title: Charles Butler’s The Feminine Monarchie, or the History of Bees (1634 edition)
content: The earliest full-length work of apiculture published in English, which popularised the discovery that bee colonies have queens instead of kings.
snippet: The earliest full-length work of apiculture published in English, which popularised the discovery that bee colonies have queens instead of kings.
